summary: McKenzie reconsiders his negative opinion of Yahoo Search Marketing (YSM/Overture) after seeing strong performance metrics. Despite the platform's terrible interface, 3-day ad approval delays, and broken analytics integration, his Yahoo ads achieved 29 cents CPA with 40% conversion rate — significantly outperforming his Google AdWords at 50 cents CPA and 20% conversion. He decides to continue spending on both platforms, allocating $30 to Yahoo and $90 to Google for the next month. The post also touches on the accounting complexity of when to recognize ad expenses.
key_points: Yahoo Search Marketing delivered dramatically better results than Google AdWords: 29 cents CPA vs 50 cents, and 40% conversion vs 20%
Platform usability and metrics performance can be completely uncorrelated — the worse platform produced better results
His target audience (teachers) appeared to be disproportionately concentrated on Yahoo rather than Google
Ad expense accounting is genuinely ambiguous: timing of click, mental commitment, or credit card charge each give different budget pictures
money_quotes: Yes, their interface is terrible. Yes, their three day delay between me putting in a new ad and it showing up makes constant refinement impossible. Yes, their integration into Analytics sucks.
But, well, numbers do not lie.
So, comparing by comparison, a week's worth of my barely-optimized Yahoo ads: 29 cents. 40% conversion. Good God.
Apparently the teachers are all over Yahoo.
Google is more fun than some games I've paid money for… cheaper, too, come to think of it.
Do you expense the ad when the click comes in? Or when you mentally commit yourself to spending X over a certain period? Or when your credit card is actually charged?
key_insight: Let performance data override your qualitative judgments about platforms — a terrible user experience for the advertiser can still deliver superior results if that's where your customers are.
